title: WLC 123-133: The Fifth Commandment — Honor Your Father and Mother (Part II)
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: 08-20-23 - WLC 123-133 - Part II - Elder Andrew Hoy.txt

outline.title: Review and Additional Thoughts on Last Week's Lesson
subpoints: Review of the DAD acronym: Disposition, Actions, Diction
Honoring parents who have died: continuing to live according to their godly instruction honors them posthumously
Parents' duty: holding children accountable to the commandment is an act of love, not imposition
Implied obligation: make it easy for your children to honor you (Chad Van Dixhorn / William Gooch)

outline.title: The Promise Attached to the Fifth Commandment
subpoints: WLC Q132: the promise of long life and prosperity as far as it serves God's glory and the person's good
Natural consequences: honoring parents and living wisely tends to produce flourishing; foolishness tends toward destruction
Personal illustration: knowing three young men who died young from foolishness
The problem of exceptions: pious children sometimes die young; disrespectful people sometimes live long
Four approaches to the exceptions: no one obeys perfectly; read it as a general truism; read it as a corporate promise to the nation; eschatological fulfillment in eternal life
Calvin / Grotius: God's promises are not void even when temporal blessings are withheld — better compensation is secured in heaven
The catechism qualifies the promise: "as far as it shall serve for God's glory and their own good"
Christians should not feel guilty about prosperity — God desires to bless his people
How to receive prosperity: as a gift from God; hold it loosely as temporal; be aware of its temptations; use it to bless others

outline.title: The Broader Application: WLC Q124 — Who Are "Father and Mother"?
subpoints: WLC Q124: "Father and mother" includes not only natural parents but all superiors in age, gifts, family, church, and commonwealth
J.J. Voss: the fifth commandment requires that all obligations in family, church, and state be performed properly and with the right attitude
Clarification: "superiors and inferiors" language describes positional authority, not intrinsic worth or dignity
Biblical basis: the Scripture uses "father" and "mother" language for multiple authority relationships
1 Timothy 5:1-2: exhort an older man as a father, older women as mothers
Genesis 4:20-22: fathers of those in a trade (superiors in gifts)
Genesis 45:8: Joseph made a father to Pharaoh (superior in gifts and position)
2 Kings 5:13: Naaman's servant calls him "my father" (superior in workplace)
2 Kings 2:12: Elisha calls Elijah "my father, my father" (spiritual superior)
Isaiah 49:23: kings as foster fathers and queens as nursing mothers (political superiors)

outline.title: WLC Q125: Why Are Superiors Styled Father and Mother?
subpoints: To teach superiors to treat their inferiors with the love and tenderness of natural parents
To motivate inferiors to obedient cheerfulness toward their superiors as toward parents
The family is the foundational institution from which all other authority relationships derive their template
Kevin DeYoung: the parent-child relationship is a template for any other relationship of authority
All authority figures should take ownership of those under them and show genuine care
Employers, elders, political leaders — all should express their authority with love and tenderness
Voss: love and tenderness do not mean going soft — it is the attitude with which discipline is exercised
God disciplines his own as a father disciplines his children (Hebrews 12)
No discipline is pleasant in the moment, but yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness

summary:
This second part of Elder Andrew Hoy's lesson on the fifth commandment continues from the previous session, covering the promise attached to the commandment and then moving into the Larger Catechism's broader application of the commandment to all authority relationships. He opens by reviewing the DAD acronym from the previous week and adding a thought from a post-service conversation with Jim Connerley: that adult children can honor departed parents by living the kind of godly life those parents raised them to live.

The substantial middle section addresses the promise — "that your days may be long in the land" — and the challenge posed by exceptions. Elder Hoy surveys four possible interpretive approaches: no one obeys perfectly so no one is guaranteed the promise; read it as a general truism; read it as a corporate promise to the people as a whole; or see its ultimate fulfillment in eternal life. He lands on Calvin's position that when temporal blessings are withheld, better compensation is secured in heaven. He also addresses the theme of prosperity more broadly, arguing that Christians should not carry lingering guilt about material blessing — God genuinely desires to prosper his people — but should receive it as a gift, hold it loosely, and use it to bless others.

The final section opens the Larger Catechism's expansive reading of the fifth commandment to cover all authority relationships (WLC Q124), giving biblical examples of Scripture's use of "father" and "mother" language for various authority figures: elders to those younger (1 Timothy 5), those superior in gifts (Genesis 4, 45), workplace superiors (2 Kings 5), spiritual fathers (2 Kings 2), and political leaders (Isaiah 49). WLC Q125 then explains why: the parent-child relationship is the foundational template for all human authority, teaching superiors to express their authority with love and tenderness and motivating inferiors toward willing obedience.
transcript:
## Opening

This morning we are continuing with our consideration of the fifth commandment using Westminster Larger Catechism as our guide.

A few weeks ago I preached at a Bible Presbyterian church and had several post-sermon conversations where people suggested things I could have added. Overall, I was encouraged — if preaching is getting people to think more deeply. After last week's lesson here, I had a similar experience. Jim Connerley and I got talking about the ongoing obligation to honor our parents even into adulthood, even after they have passed away. He pointed out that one of the greatest ways to honor our parents is to live a life that is honoring to God — especially for those raised in Christian homes, to live the kind of life that our parents raised us to live. What a great tribute and testimony. And that tribute can go on even after our parents have left this world.

## Review

This morning we continue with the fifth commandment. Can anyone remember my acronym for applying the idea of honor? DAD — Disposition, Actions, and Diction. The last D I had to stretch a little to make the acronym work.

Before we move on from the text of the commandment, I want to note implied obligations for parents as well. Many of the best, most sacrificial parents I know drop the ball in one area: they don't hold their children accountable to this commandment. They can take it when children dishonor them — they're adults, they're secure. But parents, you're doing your children a major disservice if you do not hold them accountable for the way they speak to you, the way they act toward you. The home is the training ground for the world. Children who are not held accountable for this commandment at home will carry that deficit everywhere they go.

Second: Chad Van Dixhorn, drawing from the Puritan William Gooch, notes that in Ephesians 5, all the household duties are listed together so everyone can read what is required of everyone else. One purpose is so that each person can love others by making it easier for them to do what is required of them. Parents, one way to love your children is to make it easy for them to honor you. Live honorably. Make their obedience a joy rather than a burden.

## The Promise Attached to the Fifth Commandment

I skipped over the promise last week — we still need to address it. Paul tells us in Ephesians 6 that this is "the first commandment with a promise." The WLC Q132 states: "The reasons annexed to the fifth commandment in these words, that thy days may be long upon the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee, is an express promise of long life and prosperity, as far as it shall serve for God's glory and their own good, to all such as keep this commandment."

At first consideration it seems self-evident: children who honor their parents and live wisely tend to flourish. Foolishness tends toward destruction. In the years following high school, I personally knew three young men who died from foolish decisions — and there was a common thread of disregard for parents and authority among them. Life experience seems to confirm the promise.

But there's a problem — the exceptions. Many people who were outwardly the most honoring, most pious children died young. And many disrespectful people have lived long. We want to focus on the exceptions.

Four ways to approach the exceptions:

1. No one obeys the commandment perfectly, so technically no one earns the promise. There may be some truth here, but it doesn't seem to be the spirit of the text.

2. Read it as a general truism, not an absolute promise. But as Paul calls it a promise, this doesn't quite work either.

3. Read it as a corporate promise — to the people as a whole, not necessarily to every individual. If you are a people who honor their fathers and mothers, you will be a people who live long and prosper in the land. This makes room for individual exceptions while affirming the general pattern.

4. Eschatological fulfillment: for all who truly love God and have honored their parents, the promise will be fulfilled completely in everlasting life in heaven. Calvin: "In as much as long life is not vouchsafed to all who have discharged the duties of piety toward their parents... it must be remembered that with respect to temporal rewards an infallible law is by no means laid down... a better compensation is secured in heaven for believers who have been deprived of earthly blessings."

The catechism itself qualifies the promise: "as far as it shall serve for God's glory and their own good." God reserves the right to disperse his blessings according to his eternal, perfect wisdom — for his glory and for our good. When a pious child dies young, parents can trust that it is part of God's good and perfect will.

A word on prosperity: Christians are sometimes uncomfortable with it. Part of this is a good reaction to the prosperity gospel (more faith = more money). Part may also be an overcorrection to Jesus's many warnings about wealth. But we must not forget that all blessings come from the hand of God, and that he genuinely desires to prosper his people. Joshua 1:9 and Deuteronomy 8:6-10 remind us that God desires to bring his people into a good land and give them good things — the question is how we receive and steward those gifts.

How should Christians receive prosperity?
- As a gift from God — to his glory.
- Hold it loosely — it is temporal; the greater blessings are spiritual.
- Recognize the temptations that come with wealth — Jesus warned many times that wealth can draw us away from God.
- Use it to bless others — as those who have been given prosperity, we should give ourselves and our resources for the good of others, adorning the gospel of Jesus Christ.

## WLC Q124: Who Are "Father and Mother"?

Q124: "Who are meant by father and mother in the fifth commandment?" Answer: "By father and mother in the fifth commandment are meant, not only natural parents, but all superiors in age and gifts, and especially such as, by God's ordinance, are over us in place of authority, whether in family, church, or commonwealth."

J.J. Voss summarizes: "The fifth commandment requires that all obligations in the sphere of human society — including family, church, and state — be performed properly and with the right attitude."

A clarification on the language of superiors and inferiors: this is uncomfortable language in our age, and rightly so if misunderstood. We are not speaking of intrinsic dignity or worth as human beings. This is purely about positional authority. A boss is not a better human being than an employee. An elder is not more valuable in God's sight than a new believer. A husband is not worth more than his wife. But there are positional differences — someone in a position of authority, and someone in a position of submission to that authority.

The biblical basis for this broader reading is that Scripture itself uses the language of "father" and "mother" for various authority figures:

- **Superiors in age**: 1 Timothy 5:1-2 — "Do not rebuke an older man but exhort him as a father, younger men as brothers, older women as mothers, younger women as sisters with all purity."
- **Superiors in gifts**: Genesis 4:20-22 — Jabal was "the father of those who dwell in tents and have livestock"; Jubal was "the father of all those who play the harp and flute" — a broader fatherhood to those in a trade or craft.
- **Superiors in the workplace**: Genesis 45:8 — Joseph says, "God has made me a father to Pharaoh." 2 Kings 5:13 — Naaman's servant addresses him as "my father."
- **Spiritual superiors**: 2 Kings 2:12 — Elisha crying out to Elijah as he departs: "My father, my father, the chariot of Israel and its horsemen."
- **Political superiors**: Isaiah 49:23 — "Kings shall be your foster fathers, and their queens your nursing mothers."

Even if you're not convinced that the fifth commandment directly applies to all these relationships, the Bible gives direct commands to honor each of them elsewhere: 1 Timothy 5 for age superiors; Ephesians 6:5-8 for employer-employee; 1 Peter 2:13-17 and Romans 13:1-7 for civil authorities.

## WLC Q125: Why Are Superiors Called Father and Mother?

Q125: "Why are superiors styled father and mother?" Answer: "Superiors are styled father and mother, both to teach them in all duties toward their inferiors, like natural parents, to express love and tenderness to them according to their several relations; and to work inferiors to a greater willingness and cheerfulness in performing their duties to their superiors, as to their parents."

The family is the foundational institution from which all other authority relationships derive their template. Kevin DeYoung: the parent-child relationship is "a template for any other relationship of authority in our lives." The template teaches:

For superiors: express authority with the love and tenderness of a parent toward a child. Employers — do you see your employees as people to care for and shepherd, not merely sources of labor? Elders — we are responsible to shepherd the people in the church with the care of a parent. Political leaders — (I'll just say "if only" and leave it there, as political debates are already starting).

Voss cautions: love and tenderness do not mean going soft. A judge may still sentence a criminal. Taxes may still be collected. Love and tenderness are not a substitute for justice — they are the spirit in which all duties, including hard ones, are carried out. Often the most loving thing is firm, consistent discipline.

For inferiors: seeing superiors through the lens of father and mother motivates greater willingness and cheerfulness in obedience. We are to obey our bosses, our elders, our civil authorities — not grudgingly, but with the disposition we would ideally show to loving parents.

God himself disciplines his own as a father disciplines his children. Hebrews 12 says that no discipline is pleasant in the moment, but yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness. We serve a God who as our father shapes and disciplines us for righteousness — and we should expect and receive that from the authority structures he has placed us under as well.

Next week we will look at the particular duties laid out for both superiors and inferiors in each of these realms — family, church, and state. It's a rich list that could almost be an entire sermon series.

*Closing prayer.*
